{{Lunisolar May|qq=}} '''Lunisolar May''' (旧暦五月) is the fifth {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Calendar|ja|和暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calendar|1=Japanese year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5372133}} (the {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}’s {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}).
The month that includes the {{ill|Summer solstice|en|Summer solstice|WD=Q7603898|lt=Summer Solstice}} becomes May. In the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}} ({{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}), it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|May|en|May|WD=Q119|lt=May}} to early {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July}}.
Another name for May is {{nihongo|'''Satsuki'''|さつき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|May|ja|5月|qq=draft}}.
In the {{ill|Eastern|ja|東洋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern|1=Eastern|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125474644}} {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies each year, there are years in which May ends on the 29th and May 30th does not exist.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|May (Lunisolar)|ja|5月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar May|lt_ja=旧暦5月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Satsuki''' (さつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for May of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The term “Satsuki” derives from “Sanahetsuki” (早苗月; さなへつき), meaning “month of early rice seedlings,” which was later shortened. Another theory holds that the element “sa” itself carries the meaning of rice planting, making “Satsuki” simply “month of rice planting.” Additionally, the character 皐 originally denotes rice ears offered to the gods.
In works such as the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, the characters “五月” were written and read as “Satsuki,” and it was only later that the name began to be written as 皐月. The notation “皐月” originally appears in Chinese classics as an alternate name for the fifth month of the lunisolar calendar. According to the {{ill|Erya|en|Erya|WD=Q1207685|lt=Erya}}, in {{ill|ancient China|ja|古代中国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient China|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国の歴史|1=history of China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q82972}} the twelve months were each called “陬如寎余皋且相壯玄陽辜涂” (皋 is the original character for 皐).
“Satsuki” is also the name of a flower. Another alternate name is “Ayamezuki” (菖蒲月; あやめづき).
The lunisolar May corresponds to June through July of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}} and marks the season of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=plum rain}}. “Samidare” (五月雨) is another name for the plum rain, and “Satsukibare” (五月晴れ) originally refers to the clear intervals between showers.
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, May is called “Muntukkachup” (ムントゥッカチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: mun tukka cup), meaning “month of grass growth”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
The English month name {{lang|en|May}} is said to derive from the goddess Maia ({{lang|la|Maia}}) of fertility in {{ill|Roman mythology|en|Roman mythology|WD=Q122173|lt=Roman mythology}}. Of the twelve months, it is the only one without an English abbreviation<ref group="note">English month abbreviations are three letters long; May already has three letters.</ref>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 5 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 5-ngie̍t]][[ja:5月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 5월]][[th:เดือน 5]][[za:Ndwenngux]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 5 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:五月]][[zh:农历五月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 5 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 5-ngie̍t]][[ja:5月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 5월]][[th:เดือน 5]][[za:Ndwenngux]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 5 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:五月]][[zh:农历五月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 5 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 5-ngie̍t]][[ja:5月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 5월]][[th:เดือน 5]][[za:Ndwenngux]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 5 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:五月]][[zh:农历五月]]
{{Lunisolar May|qq=}} '''Lunisolar May''' (旧暦五月) is the fifth {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Calendar|ja|和暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calendar|1=Japanese year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5372133}} (the {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}’s {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}).
The month that includes the {{ill|Summer solstice|en|Summer solstice|WD=Q7603898|lt=Summer Solstice}} becomes May. In the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}} ({{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}), it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|May|en|May|WD=Q119|lt=May}} to early {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July}}.
Another name for May is {{nihongo|'''Satsuki'''|さつき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|May|ja|5月|qq=draft}}.
In the {{ill|Eastern|ja|東洋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern|1=Eastern|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125474644}} {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies each year, there are years in which May ends on the 29th and May 30th does not exist.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|May (Lunisolar)|ja|5月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar May|lt_ja=旧暦5月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Satsuki''' (さつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for May of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The term “Satsuki” derives from “Sanahetsuki” (早苗月; さなへつき), meaning “month of early rice seedlings,” which was later shortened. Another theory holds that the element “sa” itself carries the meaning of rice planting, making “Satsuki” simply “month of rice planting.” Additionally, the character 皐 originally denotes rice ears offered to the gods.
In works such as the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, the characters “五月” were written and read as “Satsuki,” and it was only later that the name began to be written as 皐月. The notation “皐月” originally appears in Chinese classics as an alternate name for the fifth month of the lunisolar calendar. According to the {{ill|Erya|en|Erya|WD=Q1207685|lt=Erya}}, in {{ill|ancient China|ja|古代中国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient China|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国の歴史|1=history of China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q82972}} the twelve months were each called “陬如寎余皋且相壯玄陽辜涂” (皋 is the original character for 皐).
“Satsuki” is also the name of a flower. Another alternate name is “Ayamezuki” (菖蒲月; あやめづき).
The lunisolar May corresponds to June through July of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}} and marks the season of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=plum rain}}. “Samidare” (五月雨) is another name for the plum rain, and “Satsukibare” (五月晴れ) originally refers to the clear intervals between showers.
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, May is called “Muntukkachup” (ムントゥッカチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: mun tukka cup), meaning “month of grass growth”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
The English month name {{lang|en|May}} is said to derive from the goddess Maia ({{lang|la|Maia}}) of fertility in {{ill|Roman mythology|en|Roman mythology|WD=Q122173|lt=Roman mythology}}. Of the twelve months, it is the only one without an English abbreviation<ref group="note">English month abbreviations are three letters long; May already has three letters.</ref>.

{{Lunisolar May|qq=}} '''Lunisolar May''' (旧暦五月) is the fifth {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Calendar|ja|和暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calendar|1=Japanese year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5372133}} (the {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}’s {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}).
The month that includes the {{ill|Summer solstice|en|Summer solstice|WD=Q7603898|lt=Summer Solstice}} becomes May. In the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}} ({{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}), it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|May|en|May|WD=Q119|lt=May}} to early {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July}}.
Another name for May is {{nihongo|'''Satsuki'''|さつき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|May|ja|5月|qq=draft}}.
In the {{ill|Eastern|ja|東洋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern|1=Eastern|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125474644}} {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies each year, there are years in which May ends on the 29th and May 30th does not exist.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|May (Lunisolar)|ja|5月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar May|lt_ja=旧暦5月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Satsuki''' (さつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for May of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The term “Satsuki” derives from “Sanahetsuki” (早苗月; さなへつき), meaning “month of early rice seedlings,” which was later shortened. Another theory holds that the element “sa” itself carries the meaning of rice planting, making “Satsuki” simply “month of rice planting.” Additionally, the character 皐 originally denotes rice ears offered to the gods.
In works such as the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, the characters “五月” were written and read as “Satsuki,” and it was only later that the name began to be written as 皐月. The notation “皐月” originally appears in Chinese classics as an alternate name for the fifth month of the lunisolar calendar. According to the {{ill|Erya|en|Erya|WD=Q1207685|lt=Erya}}, in {{ill|ancient China|ja|古代中国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient China|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国の歴史|1=history of China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q82972}} the twelve months were each called “陬如寎余皋且相壯玄陽辜涂” (皋 is the original character for 皐).
“Satsuki” is also the name of a flower. Another alternate name is “Ayamezuki” (菖蒲月; あやめづき).
The lunisolar May corresponds to June through July of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}} and marks the season of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=plum rain}}. “Samidare” (五月雨) is another name for the plum rain, and “Satsukibare” (五月晴れ) originally refers to the clear intervals between showers.
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, May is called “Muntukkachup” (ムントゥッカチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: mun tukka cup), meaning “month of grass growth”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
The English month name {{lang|en|May}} is said to derive from the goddess Maia ({{lang|la|Maia}}) of fertility in {{ill|Roman mythology|en|Roman mythology|WD=Q122173|lt=Roman mythology}}. Of the twelve months, it is the only one without an English abbreviation<ref group="note">English month abbreviations are three letters long; May already has three letters.</ref>.

{{Lunisolar May|qq=}} '''Lunisolar May''' (旧暦五月) is the fifth {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Calendar|ja|和暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calendar|1=Japanese year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5372133}} (the {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}’s {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}).
The month that includes the {{ill|Summer solstice|en|Summer solstice|WD=Q7603898|lt=Summer Solstice|1=Summer solstice}} becomes May. In the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} ({{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}), it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|May|en|May|WD=Q119|lt=May|1=May}} to early {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July|1=July}}.
Another name for May is {{nihongo|'''Satsuki'''|さつき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|May|ja|5月|qq=draft}}.
In the {{ill|Eastern|ja|東洋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern|1=Q125474644|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125474644}} {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies each year, there are years in which May ends on the 29th and May 30th does not exist.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the {{ill|May (Lunisolar)|ja|5月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar May|lt_ja=旧暦5月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Satsuki''' (さつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for May of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The term “Satsuki” derives from “Sanahetsuki” (早苗月; さなへつき), meaning “month of early rice seedlings,” which was later shortened. Another theory holds that the element “sa” itself carries the meaning of rice planting, making “Satsuki” simply “month of rice planting.” Additionally, the character 皐 originally denotes rice ears offered to the gods.
In works such as the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, the characters “五月” were written and read as “Satsuki,” and it was only later that the name began to be written as 皐月. The notation “皐月” originally appears in Chinese classics as an alternate name for the fifth month of the lunisolar calendar. According to the {{ill|Erya|en|Erya|WD=Q1207685|lt=Erya|1=Erya}}, in {{ill|ancient China|ja|古代中国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient China|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国の歴史|1=History of China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q82972}} the twelve months were each called “陬如寎余皋且相壯玄陽辜涂” (皋 is the original character for 皐).
“Satsuki” is also the name of a flower. Another alternate name is “Ayamezuki” (菖蒲月; あやめづき).
The lunisolar May corresponds to June through July of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}} and marks the season of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=plum rain|1=East Asian rainy season}}. “Samidare” (五月雨) is another name for the plum rain, and “Satsukibare” (五月晴れ) originally refers to the clear intervals between showers.
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, May is called “Muntukkachup” (ムントゥッカチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: mun tukka cup), meaning “month of grass growth”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
The English month name {{lang|en|May}} is said to derive from the goddess Maia ({{lang|la|Maia}}) of fertility in {{ill|Roman mythology|en|Roman mythology|WD=Q122173|lt=Roman mythology|1=Roman mythology}}. Of the twelve months, it is the only one without an English abbreviation<ref group="note">English month abbreviations are three letters long; May already has three letters.</ref>.

{{Lunisolar May|qq=}} '''Lunisolar May''' (旧暦五月) is the fifth {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Calendar|ja|和暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calendar|1=Japanese year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5372133}} (the {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}’s {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}).
The month that includes the {{ill|Summer solstice|en|Summer solstice|WD=Q7603898|lt=Summer Solstice|1=Summer solstice}} becomes May. In the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} ({{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}), it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|May|en|May|WD=Q119|lt=May|1=May}} to early {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July|1=July}}.
Another name for May is {{nihongo|'''Satsuki'''|さつき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|May|ja|5月|qq=draft}}.
In the {{ill|Eastern|ja|東洋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern|1=Q125474644|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125474644}} {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies each year, there are years in which May ends on the 29th and May 30th does not exist.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the {{ill|May (Lunisolar)|ja|5月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar May|lt_ja=旧暦5月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Satsuki''' (さつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for May of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The term “Satsuki” derives from “Sanahetsuki” (早苗月; さなへつき), meaning “month of early rice seedlings,” which was later shortened. Another theory holds that the element “sa” itself carries the meaning of rice planting, making “Satsuki” simply “month of rice planting.” Additionally, the character 皐 originally denotes rice ears offered to the gods.
In works such as the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, the characters “五月” were written and read as “Satsuki,” and it was only later that the name began to be written as 皐月. The notation “皐月” originally appears in Chinese classics as an alternate name for the fifth month of the lunisolar calendar. According to the {{ill|Erya|en|Erya|WD=Q1207685|lt=Erya|1=Erya}}, in {{ill|ancient China|ja|古代中国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient China|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国の歴史|1=History of China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q82972}} the twelve months were each called “陬如寎余皋且相壯玄陽辜涂” (皋 is the original character for 皐).
“Satsuki” is also the name of a flower. Another alternate name is “Ayamezuki” (菖蒲月; あやめづき).
The lunisolar May corresponds to June through July of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}} and marks the season of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=plum rain|1=East Asian rainy season}}. “Samidare” (五月雨) is another name for the plum rain, and “Satsukibare” (五月晴れ) originally refers to the clear intervals between showers.
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, May is called “Muntukkachup” (ムントゥッカチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: mun tukka cup), meaning “month of grass growth”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
The English month name {{lang|en|May}} is said to derive from the goddess Maia ({{lang|la|Maia}}) of fertility in {{ill|Roman mythology|en|Roman mythology|WD=Q122173|lt=Roman mythology|1=Roman mythology}}. Of the twelve months, it is the only one without an English abbreviation<ref group="note">English month abbreviations are three letters long; May already has three letters.</ref>.

{{Lunisolar May|qq=}} '''Lunisolar May''' (旧暦五月) is the fifth {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Calendar|ja|和暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calendar|1=Japanese year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5372133}} (the {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}’s {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}).
The month that includes the {{ill|Summer solstice|en|Summer solstice|WD=Q7603898|lt=Summer Solstice|1=Summer solstice}} becomes May. In the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} ({{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}), it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|May|en|May|WD=Q119|lt=May|1=May}} to early {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July|1=July}}.
Another name for May is {{nihongo|'''Satsuki'''|さつき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|May|ja|5月|qq=draft}}.
In the {{ill|Eastern|ja|東洋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern|1=Q125474644|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125474644}} {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies each year, there are years in which May ends on the 29th and May 30th does not exist.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the {{ill|May (Lunisolar)|ja|5月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar May|lt_ja=旧暦5月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Satsuki''' (さつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for May of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The term “Satsuki” derives from “Sanahetsuki” (早苗月; さなへつき), meaning “month of early rice seedlings,” which was later shortened. Another theory holds that the element “sa” itself carries the meaning of rice planting, making “Satsuki” simply “month of rice planting.” Additionally, the character 皐 originally denotes rice ears offered to the gods.
In works such as the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, the characters “五月” were written and read as “Satsuki,” and it was only later that the name began to be written as 皐月. The notation “皐月” originally appears in Chinese classics as an alternate name for the fifth month of the lunisolar calendar. According to the {{ill|Erya|en|Erya|WD=Q1207685|lt=Erya|1=Erya}}, in {{ill|ancient China|ja|古代中国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient China|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国の歴史|1=History of China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q82972}} the twelve months were each called “陬如寎余皋且相壯玄陽辜涂” (皋 is the original character for 皐).
“Satsuki” is also the name of a flower. Another alternate name is “Ayamezuki” (菖蒲月; あやめづき).
The lunisolar May corresponds to June through July of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}} and marks the season of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|WD=Q658486|lt=plum rain|1=East Asian rainy season}}. “Samidare” (五月雨) is another name for the plum rain, and “Satsukibare” (五月晴れ) originally refers to the clear intervals between showers.
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, May is called “Muntukkachup” (ムントゥッカチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: mun tukka cup), meaning “month of grass growth”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
The English month name {{lang|en|May}} is said to derive from the goddess Maia ({{lang|la|Maia}}) of fertility in {{ill|Roman mythology|en|Roman mythology|WD=Q122173|lt=Roman mythology|1=Roman mythology}}. Of the twelve months, it is the only one without an English abbreviation<ref group="note">English month abbreviations are three letters long; May already has three letters.</ref>.

{{Lunisolar May|qq=}} '''Lunisolar May''' (旧暦五月) is the fifth {{ill|Month|en|Month|qid=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Calendar|ja|和暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calendar|1=Japanese year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5372133}} (the {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|qid=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}’s {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}).
The month that includes the {{ill|Summer solstice|en|Summer solstice|qid=Q7603898|lt=Summer Solstice|1=Summer solstice}} becomes May. In the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} ({{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125314718}}), it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|May|en|May|qid=Q119|lt=May|1=May}} to early {{ill|July|en|July|qid=Q121|lt=July|1=July}}.
Another name for May is {{nihongo|'''Satsuki'''|さつき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|May|ja|5月|qq=draft}}.
In the {{ill|Eastern|ja|東洋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern|1=Q125474644|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125474644}} {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|qid=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies each year, there are years in which May ends on the 29th and May 30th does not exist.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the {{ill|May (Lunisolar)|ja|5月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar May|lt_ja=旧暦5月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Satsuki''' (さつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for May of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125314718}}. The term “Satsuki” derives from “Sanahetsuki” (早苗月; さなへつき), meaning “month of early rice seedlings,” which was later shortened. Another theory holds that the element “sa” itself carries the meaning of rice planting, making “Satsuki” simply “month of rice planting.” Additionally, the character 皐 originally denotes rice ears offered to the gods.
In works such as the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, the characters “五月” were written and read as “Satsuki,” and it was only later that the name began to be written as 皐月. The notation “皐月” originally appears in Chinese classics as an alternate name for the fifth month of the lunisolar calendar. According to the {{ill|Erya|en|Erya|qid=Q1207685|lt=Erya|1=Erya}}, in {{ill|ancient China|ja|古代中国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient China|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国の歴史|1=History of China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q82972}} the twelve months were each called “陬如寎余皋且相壯玄陽辜涂” (皋 is the original character for 皐).
“Satsuki” is also the name of a flower. Another alternate name is “Ayamezuki” (菖蒲月; あやめづき).
The lunisolar May corresponds to June through July of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125314718}} and marks the season of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|qid=Q658486|lt=plum rain|1=East Asian rainy season}}. “Samidare” (五月雨) is another name for the plum rain, and “Satsukibare” (五月晴れ) originally refers to the clear intervals between showers.
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|qid=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, May is called “Muntukkachup” (ムントゥッカチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|qid=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: mun tukka cup), meaning “month of grass growth”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
The English month name {{lang|en|May}} is said to derive from the goddess Maia ({{lang|la|Maia}}) of fertility in {{ill|Roman mythology|en|Roman mythology|qid=Q122173|lt=Roman mythology|1=Roman mythology}}. Of the twelve months, it is the only one without an English abbreviation<ref group="note">English month abbreviations are three letters long; May already has three letters.</ref>.

revision.text:
{{Lunisolar May|qq=}} '''Lunisolar May''' (旧暦五月) is the fifth {{ill|Month|en|Month|qid=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Calendar|ja|和暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calendar|1=Japanese year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5372133}} (the {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|qid=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}’s {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}).
The month that includes the {{ill|Summer solstice|en|Summer solstice|qid=Q7603898|lt=Summer Solstice|1=Summer solstice}} becomes May. In the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} ({{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125314718}}), it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|May|en|May|qid=Q119|lt=May|1=May}} to early {{ill|July|en|July|qid=Q121|lt=July|1=July}}.
Another name for May is {{nihongo|'''Satsuki'''|さつき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|May|ja|5月|qq=draft}}.
In the {{ill|Eastern|ja|東洋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern|1=Q125474644|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125474644}} {{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|qid=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies each year, there are years in which May ends on the 29th and May 30th does not exist.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the {{ill|May (Lunisolar)|ja|5月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar May|lt_ja=旧暦5月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Satsuki''' (さつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for May of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125314718}}. The term “Satsuki” derives from “Sanahetsuki” (早苗月; さなへつき), meaning “month of early rice seedlings,” which was later shortened. Another theory holds that the element “sa” itself carries the meaning of rice planting, making “Satsuki” simply “month of rice planting.” Additionally, the character 皐 originally denotes rice ears offered to the gods.
In works such as the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, the characters “五月” were written and read as “Satsuki,” and it was only later that the name began to be written as 皐月. The notation “皐月” originally appears in Chinese classics as an alternate name for the fifth month of the lunisolar calendar. According to the {{ill|Erya|en|Erya|qid=Q1207685|lt=Erya|1=Erya}}, in {{ill|ancient China|ja|古代中国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient China|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国の歴史|1=History of China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q82972}} the twelve months were each called “陬如寎余皋且相壯玄陽辜涂” (皋 is the original character for 皐).
“Satsuki” is also the name of a flower. Another alternate name is “Ayamezuki” (菖蒲月; あやめづき).
The lunisolar May corresponds to June through July of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125314718}} and marks the season of the {{ill|East Asian rainy season|en|East Asian rainy season|qid=Q658486|lt=plum rain|1=East Asian rainy season}}. “Samidare” (五月雨) is another name for the plum rain, and “Satsukibare” (五月晴れ) originally refers to the clear intervals between showers.
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|qid=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, May is called “Muntukkachup” (ムントゥッカチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|qid=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: mun tukka cup), meaning “month of grass growth”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
The English month name {{lang|en|May}} is said to derive from the goddess Maia ({{lang|la|Maia}}) of fertility in {{ill|Roman mythology|en|Roman mythology|qid=Q122173|lt=Roman mythology|1=Roman mythology}}. Of the twelve months, it is the only one without an English abbreviation<ref group="note">English month abbreviations are three letters long; May already has three letters.</ref>.
